name: AppSignal
what_it_does: AppSignal is an all-in-one APM platform providing error monitoring, performance tracing, logging, and alerting for Ruby, Elixir, and Node.js applications via a unified REST API.
use_cases: Query recent errors and exception backtraces in a Ruby app to diagnose production incidents
Retrieve performance metrics and slow transaction traces to identify bottlenecks in API endpoints
Fetch log lines from a specific time window to correlate errors with deployment events
Create and update anomaly detection triggers and alert thresholds programmatically for automated ops
Pull host and process metrics (CPU, memory, throughput) to feed into a custom observability dashboard
not_for: Monitoring Python, Go, or Java applications — AppSignal has no official agents for those languages
Full distributed tracing across polyglot microservices — coverage is limited to supported runtimes
Log aggregation at petabyte scale — AppSignal logging is supplemental, not a Splunk/Loki replacement
best_when: Your stack is primarily Ruby on Rails, Phoenix/Elixir, or Node.js and you want error monitoring, APM, and basic logging from a single low-overhead vendor.
avoid_when: Your services are primarily Go, Python, Java, or .NET, or you need deep distributed tracing with OpenTelemetry-native tooling across many languages.

auth.notes: API key is scoped per app or per organisation token; passed as a query param or Authorization header depending on endpoint.

pricing.free_tier_limits: 1 app, limited error and performance data retention
pricing.paid_tiers: Starter ~$18/mo; Growth and Business tiers scale by host count and data volume
pricing.notes: Pricing is per app/host combination; self-hosted option is not available — SaaS only.

performance.rate_limits: Not publicly documented; practical limits observed around 100 req/min per token

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: API errors return JSON with a message field but HTTP status codes are sometimes inconsistently applied (e.g., 200 with error body).
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read endpoints are fully idempotent; write endpoints (alerts, markers) lack idempotency keys so retries can create duplicates.
known_agent_gotchas: API key scope differs between app-level and org-level tokens — using the wrong one silently returns empty results rather than an auth error
Timestamps in responses use Unix epoch integers, not ISO 8601 — agents must convert before display or comparison
Rate limits are not returned in response headers, so agents cannot implement proactive backoff
Some endpoints require the internal numeric app ID, not the app slug shown in the UI — discovery requires a separate listing call
Webhook payloads are unsigned; agents ingesting webhooks cannot verify authenticity without network-level controls

security_score_components.security_notes: API keys have no expiry mechanism by default; no IP allowlist for API access; GDPR-compliant EU data residency available.
